intro.kicker: Topic 7 of 8 • Chapter: Surface Tension • Unit: Properties of Matter • Physics
intro.description: The phenomenon where a liquid in a capillary tube either ascends or descends relative to the surrounding liquid, caused by pressure difference across curved liquid surface. This topic covers Definition and Phenomenon, Ascent Formula, Capillary Rise in Different Cases and Shape of Drops. Key principles include: Root cause: pressure difference on concave and convex curved surfaces; Occurs in capillary tubes with narrow bore. In NEET Physics, Capillarity is tested directly and as part of multi-concept problems. Mastery requires both conceptual clarity and strong numerical practice.

expectedQuestions.description: Capillarity features in NEET Physics as a direct question or embedded in multi-step problems. Detailed study of Surface Tension is recommended.

timeRequired.description: Theory 10–15 min, plus solving 30 practice problems. Efficient if you know the formula derivation.

difficulty.description: Surface Tension difficulty level. Mastery requires understanding conditions of validity, dimensional analysis, and numerical practice with NEET-style traps.

nriUsaGap.description: Capillarity under Properties of Matter receives minimal AP Physics coverage. NEET dedicates significant weightage here. NRI/US-curriculum students must study this chapter in full.

bulletNotes: Capillarity appears regularly in NEET Physics; never skip it.
Often combined with adjacent topics in multi-step problems.
Master formula derivation and conceptual application for best results.

strategySteps.title: Concept First
strategySteps.description: Understand the physical meaning behind Capillarity. Derive key formulae from first principles so you can reconstruct them under exam pressure.

strategySteps.title: Formula Mapping
strategySteps.description: List all formulae for this topic on a single sheet. Note the conditions under which each applies. NEET frequently tests boundary conditions and exceptions.

strategySteps.title: Practice NEET-style MCQs
strategySteps.description: Solve at least 20 past-paper-style questions on this topic. Note the traps: unit mismatches, sign errors, and direction conventions account for most wrong answers.

cards.title: Core Concept: Capillarity
cards.coreRevision: Capillarity is a key pillar of Surface Tension in NEET Physics. Understanding its definitions, derivations, and standard results is essential for solving both theory and numerical questions.
points: Root cause: pressure difference on concave and convex curved surfaces
Occurs in capillary tubes with narrow bore
Wetting liquids rise; non-wetting liquids fall
cards.example: Formula: h = (2T cosθ)/(rdg), ascent formula for capillary rise

cards.title: NEET Traps to Avoid
cards.coreRevision: NEET setters target conceptual gaps in Capillarity. The most common errors involve sign conventions, unit mismatches, and misapplication of conditions under which formulae are valid.
points: Root cause: pressure difference on concave and convex curved surfaces
U-tube unequal radii height difference: h = (2T cosθ/dg)[1/r₁ - 1/r₂]
cards.example: In NEET, Capillarity questions often combine with adjacent chapter concepts. Practice multi-step problems to expose hidden links.

curriculum.hintText: Topics in Capillarity covered differently or not at all in AP Physics vs NEET.

gaps.title: Capillarity in US vs Indian Curriculum
gaps.description: Properties of matter (elasticity, surface tension, fluid mechanics) receive minimal coverage in AP Physics 1. NEET dedicates significant weightage to viscosity, Bernoulli, surface energy, and capillarity.
bullets: NEET expects derivation-level mastery; AP Physics often treats Capillarity as applied only.
NRI students should supplement textbook study with NEET-specific problem sets.
Pay special attention to SI unit standards and sign conventions which differ from US conventions.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Capillarity in the context of NEET Physics?

options.text: Capillarity applies only under idealized conditions with no friction or air resistance
Capillarity is a fundamental result derivable from first principles and applies within well-defined physical conditions
Capillarity has no mathematical formulation and is purely qualitative
Capillarity contradicts Newton's laws in non-inertial frames

questions.explanation: Capillarity is a rigorously derived physical principle within Surface Tension. It has a precise mathematical form, applies under specified conditions, and is fully consistent with Newton's laws. NEET frequently tests whether students can distinguish domain of applicability from physical principle validity.

questions.question: In the study of Surface Tension, which statement about Ascent Formula is CORRECT for a NEET Physics student?

options.text: It is only tested as an isolated topic and never combined with energy or momentum problems
The dimensional formula does not follow from SI base units
It is integral to Surface Tension and appears in direct and multi-concept NEET questions
It is outside the NEET Physics syllabus since 2020

questions.explanation: Ascent Formula is a core component of Surface Tension and regularly appears in NEET. Multi-step problems combining this with energy, momentum, or kinematics are common. Dimensional analysis confirms SI unit consistency. This topic has remained in the NEET syllabus continuously.

questions.question: Which principle related to Capillarity states: 'Root cause: pressure difference on concave and convex curved surfaces'?
options: This is the defining principle of Capillarity
This is a corollary, not a principle of Capillarity
This applies only to rigid-body systems
questions.answer: This is the defining principle of Capillarity

questions.question: In a NEET context, when is Capillarity most directly applicable?
options: Only in ideal, frictionless, zero-gravity scenarios
Whenever the stated conditions for Capillarity are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems
questions.answer: Whenever the stated conditions for Capillarity are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the dimensional formula of the primary physical quantity in Capillarity?
options: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation
It requires non-SI units for proper expression
Dimensional analysis is inapplicable to this quantity
questions.answer: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation

questions.question: The most common NEET trap in Capillarity problems is:
options: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions
questions.answer: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions

items.title: Define Capillarity precisely
items.description: Write out the complete definition of Capillarity including all qualifying conditions and the physical quantities involved.

items.title: Derive the key formula from first principles
items.description: Reproduce the main derivation without notes. Understanding each step ensures you can identify which formula applies under non-standard NEET conditions.

items.title: State conditions of validity
items.description: List all assumptions and conditions under which Capillarity results hold. NEET setters test these boundaries.

items.title: Master Definition and Phenomenon
items.description: Solve at least 5 NEET-style problems specifically on Definition and Phenomenon to ensure pattern recognition and speed.

items.title: Master Ascent Formula
items.description: Solve at least 5 NEET-style problems specifically on Ascent Formula to ensure pattern recognition and speed.

items.question: How many questions from Capillarity appear in NEET each year?
items.answer: Capillarity typically contributes 0–2 questions per NEET paper depending on the year. While it may not appear every year, it often features in multi-concept problems linked to adjacent topics in Surface Tension. Skipping it is risky.

items.question: Is Capillarity in the NEET 2025 syllabus?
items.answer: Yes, Capillarity remains part of the NEET Physics syllabus under Surface Tension. The National Medical Commission periodically updates the syllabus, so always verify with the latest NMC/NTA official syllabus notification before your exam.

items.question: What is the best way to revise Capillarity before NEET?
items.answer: Use a three-pass strategy: (1) Read the derivation and concept; (2) Solve 10 NEET-style MCQs; (3) On the day before exam, review only the formulae sheet and your flagged error patterns from practice. Focus on conditions of validity and sign conventions.

items.question: What is the most common mistake in Capillarity problems?
items.answer: The most frequent errors in Capillarity are: applying the formula outside its domain (e.g., using ideal-gas results for real gases), sign errors in vector quantities, and ignoring units in multi-step calculations. Practice specifically designed trap questions to inoculate against these errors.

grounding_summary: Capillarity is covered in the assigned page range of pages/physics-1/ as part of Surface Tension.
The local OCR pages establish definitions, key concepts, and formulae for Capillarity in the NEET Physics context.
Chapter research from Exa confirms current NEET exam patterns and authoritative sources for Capillarity.
grounding_excerpt: By knowing the direction of resultant force we can find out the shape of meniscus because the free surface of the liquid adjust itself at right angle to this resultant force.
i.e. the resultant force directed inside the liquid. Hence the liquid meniscus must be convex upward.  |
Liquid does not wet the solid surface.
(ii) It is particular for a given pair of liquid and solid. And also on surrounding medium.
